lens.included_activities: Private lower-middle-market operators repeatedly transporting elderly, disabled, infirm, or otherwise special-needs passengers, including nonemergency medical transportation and contracted paratransit, using accessible or conventional passenger vehicles.
lens.excluded_activities: Ambulance services with medical care, school or employee bus transportation, public transit agencies, captive fleets, nonprofits without transferable control economics, pure brokers or software vendors, and occasional point-to-point operators outside special-needs transportation.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring and repeat trips paid by Medicaid agencies, managed-care plans, transportation brokers, transit authorities, care institutions, and private-pay passengers; revenue commonly follows awarded contracts or authorized fee-for-service trips, mileage, and vehicle/service categories.

a.bridge: May 2023 BLS OEWS measures the broader four-digit NAICS 4859 population, where transportation occupations are 74.40% of employment, passenger-vehicle drivers 64.64%, and office/administrative support 15.10%. I treat most driving and passenger-assistance time as not substitutable within five years, while exposing portions of scheduling, dispatch, intake, documentation, billing, and supervision. The broader industry includes shuttle services and may overstate ordinary driving and understate special-needs assistance, so it likely biases exposure upward.
a.rationale: The service is dominated by physical driving and passenger handling, but repeat trip intake, route construction, dispatch exceptions, notifications, documentation, claims preparation, and administrative follow-up create a material digital-work layer. DOT evidence that continuous dynamic optimization addresses unassigned trips and same-day productivity supports exposure in scheduling and dispatch, not driver replacement.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary from 20-30 private NEMT operators, split among drivers, dispatchers, billers, call-center staff, and managers, plus payroll by occupation, would materially tighten the range.
caveats: OEWS is at NAICS 4859 rather than 485991 and includes materially different shuttle activities.
Employment shares are used as a bridge to wage-weighted task shares; no directly measured AI-task exposure exists for this industry.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.

rho.bridge: A 2022 DOT-sponsored study of 24 transit agencies found 11 using continuous dynamic optimization in daily ADA paratransit operations and documented varied technologies and experiences. I use this as evidence that automated scheduling and dispatch are operationally feasible, then discount for private LMM operators' integration, data-quality, compliance, staff-change, and exception-handling constraints. Public agencies may have more complex procurement but more implementation resources, making the net error direction uncertain.
rho.rationale: Established routing and dispatch automation should let operators implement more than half of the exposed administrative opportunity over five years, but messy broker feeds, HIPAA-sensitive records, driver and passenger exceptions, claims rules, and thin management capacity prevent full realization.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor hours, error rates, on-time performance, denied claims, and dispatcher staffing from private NEMT deployments of AI-assisted scheduling, intake, billing, and call handling would materially move this estimate.
caveats: The adoption evidence concerns public ADA paratransit agencies, not private LMM NEMT firms.
Continuous dynamic optimization is narrower than the full set of AI-enabled workflows considered here.
Implementation varies with payer portals, broker interfaces, state rules, and fleet dispatch practices.

e.rationale: Most scaled private special-needs carriers provide repeat outsourced trips and therefore fit the lens. The range excludes firms that are owner-dependent, lack durable licenses or payer relationships, operate largely captive or grant-funded services, have weak compliance records, or cannot support a control transaction.
e.change_evidence: A coded census of the 197 frozen LMM-band firms using payer mix, recurring-contract share, ownership type, licensing status, fleet condition, and owner dependence would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: No source measures lens eligibility among LMM firms in NAICS 485991.
NAICS 485991 includes nonmedical special-needs and pet transportation as well as NEMT and paratransit.
The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate from SUSB size classes rather than observed EBITDA-band firms.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 US survey found that 22% of owners of employer businesses planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I apply that observed employer-business planning rate to eligible LMM special-needs transportation firms and widen the range for industry-specific owner age, execution risk, and unplanned transfers. Plans may overstate completed qualifying control transfers, while unplanned sales may offset part of that bias; net direction is uncertain.
s5.rationale: Employer businesses are a closer population than all small businesses, and 22% provides a defensible five-year anchor. Fleet condition, insurance, compliance history, payer credentialing, and owner-held relationships can prevent an intended succession from becoming a transferable control deal.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed private-company transactions and failed listings for NAICS 485991, linked to owner age and transfer type, would replace the cross-industry survey proxy.
caveats: The Gallup statistic covers employer businesses across industries and business sizes.
Stated plans are not completed transfers and include gifts or rare public offerings.
Internal reorganizations and closures are excluded from the target construct.

q.rationale: An operator can initially retain savings under fixed awarded rates or contracts, but Medicaid and broker channels emphasize economy, competitive bids, audits, authorized trips, and payment rules such as loaded mileage. Renewal repricing and payer bargaining should therefore share a substantial portion of gross benefit, while differentiated reliability and capacity allow some retention.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level gross-margin cohorts before and after workflow automation, including renewal rates, broker repricing, denied claims, and bid spreads across major state markets, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Retention differs sharply among fixed-fee contracts, state fee schedules, broker subcontracts, and private-pay trips.
No source directly measures pass-through of automation savings in special-needs transportation.
This primitive excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty, which are represented elsewhere.

d5.bridge: BLS projects passenger-vehicle-driver employment to rise 4.7% from 2024 to 2034, while Census reports the US population age 65 and older rose 3.1% to 61.2 million from 2023 to 2024. I use the occupational projection as a broad transport-demand anchor and aging as a directional special-needs demand driver, translating to a five-year quantity ratio centered near 1.05 rather than extrapolating the one-year aging rate. Driver employment can diverge from constant-quality service quantity through productivity, and the broad occupation includes taxis, school buses, and transit; direction of error is uncertain.
d5.rationale: Aging and durable healthcare access needs support modest real quantity growth, while payer budgets, shared rides, telehealth, family transport, and route optimization restrain it. The range allows near-flat demand if funding pressure offsets demographics and stronger growth where aging and outsourced capacity dominate.
d5.change_evidence: A payer-weighted five-year forecast of authorized and completed NEMT and paratransit trips, unique riders, denials, telehealth substitution, and constant-quality service miles by state would materially tighten the estimate.
caveats: Neither source directly measures constant-price, constant-quality quantity for private NAICS 485991 operators.
National aging does not map one-for-one to transportation eligibility or utilization.
Public ADA paratransit obligations and Medicaid transportation assurances do not guarantee private-provider volumes or adequate funding.

o.rationale: Nearly all year-five service quantity still requires an accountable transport operator because passengers must be physically moved and may need accessible vehicles, qualified drivers, boarding help, safety oversight, and documented compliance. Software, ride-sourcing, family transport, and transit integration can eliminate or self-serve some trips, but do not remove operator accountability for most special-needs rides within five years.
o.change_evidence: Payer and broker trip data identifying five-year shifts from credentialed special-needs carriers to family reimbursement, public transit, ride-hail, telehealth, or fully autonomous service would directly measure operator displacement.
caveats: No source directly estimates five-year operator-required share.
Autonomous accessible-vehicle deployment and regulatory acceptance are uncertain.
The estimate concerns retention of an accountable operator, not retention of every current driver or administrative task.

narrative.executive_view: Special-needs transportation combines durable, often mandated trip demand with a highly physical driver base. The practical AI opportunity is concentrated in scheduling, dispatch, intake, billing, documentation, and exception management rather than replacing the ride itself.
narrative.ai_impact: AI-assisted intake can structure trip requests, optimize manifests, predict lateness and no-shows, surface compliance exceptions, prepare claims, and support dispatchers. Human review remains important when mobility equipment, caregiver accompaniment, same-day changes, passenger vulnerability, or payer authorization rules make a trip nonstandard.
narrative.value_capture: Savings can be retained during fixed-rate contract periods and converted into capacity, reliability, or margin. Retention is constrained by competitive procurement, administered reimbursement, broker leverage, audits, renewal repricing, and rules that can deny payment for no-shows, unloaded miles, or documentation failures.
narrative.firm_availability: Many scaled operators should fit a repeat outsourced-service lens, but transferability depends on licenses, insurance, vehicle quality, payer credentials, compliance history, and whether customer and dispatcher relationships survive the owner. Cross-industry employer-owner evidence suggests meaningful but not abundant five-year transfer flow.
narrative.demand_durability: An aging population, Medicaid's statutory transportation assurance, and ADA complementary-paratransit duties support the need for transport. Telehealth, ride sharing, family reimbursement, public transit coordination, and funding pressure can temper trip growth, but most remaining rides still require a physically accountable operator.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The sharpest uncertainties are the absence of six-digit occupation data, uneven state reimbursement and broker practices, unknown private-firm technology baselines, insurance and driver constraints, fraud and audit exposure, and the gap between owner transfer plans and completed control deals. The industry would be unattractive where payer rates fail to cover labor and vehicle costs or where contracts can immediately reclaim efficiency gains.
narrative.principal_driver: Durable physical transport obligations paired with automatable scheduling, dispatch, billing, and compliance workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Payer and broker pricing power can absorb efficiency gains while most labor remains tied to human driving and passenger assistance.

sources.title: Other Transit and Ground Passenger Transportation — May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Broader NAICS 4859 occupation mix: transportation occupations 74.40%, passenger-vehicle drivers 64.64%, shuttle drivers/chauffeurs 52.75%, and office/administrative support 15.10% of employment.

sources.title: Consider Technology Vendor Sustainability Factors When Considering Deployment of New Automated Scheduling/Dispatching Services for ADA Paratransit Operations
sources.supports: DOT summary of a 2022 study in which 11 of 24 surveyed transit agencies used continuous dynamic optimization daily for ADA paratransit; the technology addressed unassigned trips and same-day productivity and performance.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall-2024 US survey: 22% of employer-business owners reported plans to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9% of nonemployers.

sources.title: Assurance of Transportation
sources.supports: Federal regulations and statute require state Medicaid plans to assure necessary transportation, with NEMT payment methods consistent with efficiency, economy, and quality of care.

sources.title: Medicaid Non-Emergency Medical Transportation Booklet for Providers
sources.supports: Documents broker, managed-care, and direct-vendor delivery models; competitive broker selection; state preauthorization variation; qualified-driver requirements; and payment considerations including loaded mileage and no-shows.

sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics, 2024–2034
sources.supports: BLS projects passenger-vehicle-driver employment from 994,000 in 2024 to 1,040,600 in 2034, a 4.7% increase; shuttle drivers and chauffeurs are projected to grow 6.7%.

sources.title: Part 37—Transportation Services for Individuals with Disabilities
sources.supports: Requires public fixed-route entities to provide comparable complementary paratransit to eligible individuals and describes service, eligibility, coordination, and efficiency requirements.

sources.title: Older Adults Outnumber Children in 11 States and Nearly Half of U.S. Counties
sources.supports: The US population age 65 and older rose 3.1% to 61.2 million from 2023 to 2024, reaching 18.0% of the population.
